Returns the probability that a standard normal variable falls between 0 and z - equal to Φ(z) − 0.5. Use it when you need the one-sided probability from the mean to a specific z-score.

Example: =GAUSS(1) returns approximately 0.3413.
GEOMEAN:
Returns the geometric mean of a set of positive numbers. Use it to average rates of change or growth rates - for instance, the average annual return of an investment, where the geometric mean correctly accounts for compounding.

Example: =GEOMEAN(1.05, 1.08, 0.97) returns the average growth factor.
HARMEAN:
Returns the harmonic mean of a set of numbers. Use it to average rates - speeds, prices per unit, or yields - where the reciprocal relationship matters more than simple averaging.

Example: =HARMEAN(A2:A10) returns the harmonic average.
HYPGEOM.DIST:
Returns the hypergeometric distribution probability - the probability of exactly k successes when drawing n items without replacement from a population of N containing K successes. Use it for quality sampling when items are not returned after each draw.

Example: =HYPGEOM.DIST(3, 10, 20, 100, FALSE) returns the exact probability.
INTERCEPT:
Returns the y-intercept of the linear regression line through a set of data points. Use it with SLOPE to build a simple linear model: predicted_y = SLOPE × x + INTERCEPT.

Example: =INTERCEPT(B2:B20, A2:A20) returns the constant term of the best-fit line.
KURT:
Returns the kurtosis of a dataset - a measure of how heavy the tails are compared to a normal distribution. Positive kurtosis means more extreme outliers than normal; negative means fewer. Use it to understand whether your data has unusual outlier behavior.

Example: =KURT(A2:A100) returns the excess kurtosis.
LARGE:
Returns the k-th largest value in a dataset. Use it to find top-N values - the second-highest score, third-largest sale - without sorting the data.

Example: =LARGE(A2:A100, 3) returns the third-largest value.
LOGNORM.DIST:
Returns the log-normal distribution probability (CDF or PDF). Use it to model data that is the product of many positive factors - stock prices, income distributions, particle sizes.

Example: =LOGNORM.DIST(4, 1, 0.5, TRUE) returns the cumulative probability.
LOGNORM.INV:
Returns the inverse of the log-normal cumulative distribution - the value at a given percentile. Use it to find percentile values for a log-normally distributed variable.

Example: =LOGNORM.INV(0.9, 1, 0.5) returns the 90th percentile.
MAXA:
Returns the largest value in a range, treating text as 0 and TRUE as 1. Use it when you want the maximum across a mixed-type range and need non-numeric values to be counted rather than skipped.

Example: =MAXA(A2:A20) returns the maximum even if the range contains text.
MAXIFS:
Returns the maximum value among cells that meet one or more conditions. Use it to find the highest value within a filtered group - for instance, the largest sale in the West region.

Example: =MAXIFS(C2:C100, B2:B100, "West") returns the largest value in C where B is "West".
MEDIAN:
Returns the middle value of a sorted dataset - half the values are above it and half below. Use it as a more outlier-resistant measure of center than the average.

Example: =MEDIAN(A2:A100) returns the midpoint value.
MINA:
Returns the smallest value in a range, treating text as 0 and FALSE as 0. The counterpart of MAXA - use it when non-numeric entries should be considered in the minimum calculation.

Example: =MINA(A2:A20) returns the minimum including any text (as 0) or FALSE values.
MINIFS:
Returns the minimum value among cells that meet one or more conditions. Use it to find the lowest value within a filtered subset - the smallest order, earliest date within a category.

Example: =MINIFS(C2:C100, B2:B100, "West") returns the smallest value in C where B is "West".
NEGBINOM.DIST:
Returns the negative binomial distribution probability - the probability of exactly k failures before achieving the r-th success. Use it in reliability testing and sequential sampling scenarios.

Example: =NEGBINOM.DIST(5, 3, 0.5, FALSE) returns the probability of 5 failures before the 3rd success.
NORM.DIST:
Returns the normal distribution probability (CDF or PDF) for a given mean and standard deviation. Use it to find how likely a value is under a normal distribution - for instance, what fraction of products fall within spec.

Example: =NORM.DIST(75, 70, 10, TRUE) returns P(X ≤ 75) for a N(70, 10) distribution.
NORM.INV:
Returns the inverse of the normal CDF - the value at a given cumulative probability. Use it to find percentile values for any normal distribution.

Example: =NORM.INV(0.95, 70, 10) returns the 95th percentile of a N(70, 10) distribution.
NORM.S.DIST:
Returns the standard normal distribution probability (mean=0, standard deviation=1). Use it for z-table lookups - finding the probability that a standard normal variable is at most z.

Example: =NORM.S.DIST(1.96, TRUE) returns approximately 0.975.
NORM.S.INV:
Returns the inverse of the standard normal CDF - the z-score for a given cumulative probability. Use it to find critical z-values for confidence intervals and hypothesis tests.

Example: =NORM.S.INV(0.975) returns approximately 1.96.
PEARSON:
Returns the Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient between two datasets. Functionally identical to CORREL - use either one to measure the linear relationship between two variables.

Example: =PEARSON(A2:A50, B2:B50) returns the correlation coefficient.
PHI:
Returns the value of the standard normal probability density function at a given z-value. Use it in mathematical and statistical derivations where the normal PDF is needed directly.

Example: =PHI(0) returns approximately 0.3989 (the peak of the bell curve).
POISSON.DIST:
Returns the Poisson distribution probability for a given number of events and average rate. Use it to model the probability of a count of rare, independent events - customer arrivals, server requests, or defects per unit.

Example: =POISSON.DIST(3, 5, FALSE) returns P(X=3) when the average is 5 events.
RANK.AVG:
Returns the rank of a number within a list, averaging the rank when ties occur. Use it for fair ranking where you want tied values to share the average of their positions rather than one getting an arbitrary advantage.

Example: =RANK.AVG(A2, A$2:A$100, 0) returns A2's rank in descending order.
RANK.EQ:
Returns the rank of a number within a list; all tied values receive the top rank in the group. Use it for traditional ranking where tied values claim the same (highest) position in the tie.

Example: =RANK.EQ(A2, A$2:A$100, 0) returns A2's descending rank.
RSQ:
Returns the square of the Pearson correlation coefficient (R²) - the proportion of variance in y explained by x. Use it to evaluate how well a linear regression fits your data; R²=1 means a perfect fit.

Example: =RSQ(B2:B20, A2:A20) returns R² for the linear relationship.
SKEW:
Returns the skewness of a distribution - a measure of asymmetry around the mean. Positive skew means a longer right tail; negative means a longer left tail. Use it to check whether data is approximately symmetric before applying methods that assume normality.

Example: =SKEW(A2:A100) returns the sample skewness.
SKEW.P:
Returns the population skewness, dividing by n instead of n−1. Use it when your data represents the entire population rather than a sample.

Example: =SKEW.P(A2:A100).
SLOPE:
Returns the slope of the linear regression line through a set of data points - how much y changes for each unit increase in x. Use it with INTERCEPT to build a simple linear model for prediction or trend analysis.

Example: =SLOPE(B2:B20, A2:A20) returns the slope coefficient.
SMALL:
Returns the k-th smallest value in a dataset. Use it to find bottom-N values - the second-lowest, third-cheapest - without sorting the data.

Example: =SMALL(A2:A100, 2) returns the second-smallest value.
STANDARDIZE:
Returns the standardized (z-score) equivalent of a value: (x − mean) / standard_deviation. Use it to convert raw values to standard deviations from the mean for comparison across different scales or distributions.

Example: =STANDARDIZE(A2, AVERAGE(A$2:A$100), STDEV(A$2:A$100)) returns the z-score.
STDEV.P:
Returns the population standard deviation (divides by n). Use it when your values represent the entire population and you want the exact spread of that population.

Example: =STDEV.P(A2:A100).
STDEV.S:
Returns the sample standard deviation (divides by n−1). The most common standard deviation function - use it when your data is a sample from a larger population and you want an unbiased estimate of the population spread.

Example: =STDEV.S(A2:A20).
STDEVA:
Like STDEV.S but includes text (as 0) and logical values in the calculation. Use it when your range has mixed types and non-numeric entries should contribute to the spread.

Example: =STDEVA(A2:A20).
STDEVPA:
Like STDEV.P but includes text and logical values. Use it for population standard deviation over a mixed-type range.

Example: =STDEVPA(A2:A100).
STEYX:
Returns the standard error of the predicted y values in a linear regression - a measure of how accurate the regression line's predictions are on average. Use it to gauge the precision of your linear model.

Example: =STEYX(B2:B20, A2:A20) returns the standard error of the estimate.
T.DIST:
Returns the Student's t-distribution probability (left-tailed CDF or PDF). Use it in hypothesis testing for small samples when the population standard deviation is unknown.

Example: =T.DIST(2.0, 10, TRUE) returns the left-tail probability with 10 degrees of freedom.
T.DIST.2T:
Returns the two-tailed t-distribution probability for a given t-statistic. Use it for two-tailed t-tests - when you're testing whether a difference is non-zero in either direction.

Example: =T.DIST.2T(2.0, 10) returns the two-tailed p-value.
T.DIST.RT:
Returns the right-tailed t-distribution probability. Use it for one-tailed t-tests where you're testing whether a value is greater than a null hypothesis value.

Example: =T.DIST.RT(2.0, 10) returns the right-tail p-value.
T.INV:
Returns the inverse of the left-tailed t-distribution - the t-value at a given probability. Use it to find critical values for one-tailed tests or to construct confidence intervals.

Example: =T.INV(0.95, 10) returns the one-tailed 95th-percentile t-value.
T.INV.2T:
Returns the inverse of the two-tailed t-distribution. Use it to find the critical t-value for a two-tailed hypothesis test at a given significance level.

Example: =T.INV.2T(0.05, 10) returns the critical value for a 5% two-tailed test with 10 degrees of freedom.
T.TEST:
Returns the p-value for a Student's t-test comparing two datasets. Use it to determine whether the difference in means between two groups is statistically significant.

Example: =T.TEST(A2:A20, B2:B20, 2, 2) performs a two-tailed, two-sample t-test.
VAR.P:
Returns the population variance (divides by n). Use it when your values are the entire population and you want the average squared deviation from the mean.

Example: =VAR.P(A2:A100).
VAR.S:
Returns the sample variance (divides by n−1). The most common variance function - use it when your data is a sample from a larger population.

Example: =VAR.S(A2:A20).
VARA:
Like VAR.S but includes text (as 0) and logical values. Use it when your range has mixed types and non-numeric entries should be counted.

Example: =VARA(A2:A20).
VARPA:
Like VAR.P but includes text and logical values. Use it for population variance over a mixed-type range.

Example: =VARPA(A2:A100).
WEIBULL.DIST:
Returns the Weibull distribution probability (CDF or PDF). Use it to model time-to-failure or reliability data - the Weibull distribution is widely used in engineering for survival and failure analysis.

Example: =WEIBULL.DIST(100, 1.5, 200, TRUE) returns the probability that a component fails by time 100.
Z.TEST:
Returns the one-tailed p-value of a z-test - the probability that the sample mean is at least as extreme as observed, given a hypothesized population mean. Use it to test whether a sample mean is consistent with a known or assumed population mean.

Example: =Z.TEST(A2:A30, 50, 10) tests the sample against a hypothesized mean of 50 with standard deviation 10.

ARRAYTOTEXT:
Converts an array or range to a text string, either in a concise display format or a strict parseable format. Use it when you want to show the contents of a spilled array as a single readable cell entry.

Example: =ARRAYTOTEXT(A2:A5, 1) returns "{value1,value2,value3,value4}".
ASC:
Converts full-width (double-byte) characters to half-width (single-byte) equivalents in East Asian text. Use it to normalize text that may contain full-width characters entered via Japanese or Chinese input.

Example: =ASC("ＡＢＣＤ") returns the half-width "ABCD".
CHAR:
Returns the character corresponding to a given character code (ASCII or Unicode). Use it to insert special characters - a line break (CHAR(10)), a tab (CHAR(9)), or any character that can't be typed directly - into text formulas.

Example: =A2&CHAR(10)&B2 creates a two-line cell entry.
CLEAN:
Removes non-printable characters (the first 32 ASCII control characters) from a text string. Use it to scrub imported data that contains hidden control characters causing display or formula problems.

Example: =CLEAN(A2) strips any control characters from the text.
CODE:
Returns the numeric character code of the first character in a text string. The inverse of CHAR - use it to find the code for an unusual character so you can reference it with CHAR or identify unexpected characters in data.

Example: =CODE("A") returns 65.
CONCAT:
Joins multiple text strings or ranges together into one string, with no separator between them. Use it to combine text from several cells - for instance, assembling a full name from first-name and last-name columns.

Example: =CONCAT(A2, " ", B2) joins first and last names with a space.
CONCATENATE:
Joins text strings together - an older function superseded by CONCAT. CONCAT is preferred because it also accepts ranges, while CONCATENATE requires individual cell references listed one by one.

Example: =CONCATENATE(A2, " ", B2).
DOLLAR:
Converts a number to text formatted as currency, with a specified number of decimal places and a dollar sign. Use it to embed a formatted currency value inside a text string - for example, in a generated message.

Example: =DOLLAR(1234.5, 2) returns "$1,234.50".
EXACT:
Compares two text strings and returns TRUE only if they are character-for-character identical, including case. Use it when case-sensitivity matters - "Apple" and "apple" are not equal under EXACT even though = treats them as the same.

Example: =EXACT("Apple", "apple") returns FALSE.
FIND:
Returns the position of one text string within another, case-sensitively. Use it to locate exactly where a substring begins - then combine with MID or LEFT to extract the relevant portion.

Example: =FIND("@", A2) returns the position of the "@" in an email address.
FINDB:
Like FIND but counts positions in bytes rather than characters - relevant for double-byte East Asian character sets. Use it when working with mixed single- and double-byte text in CJK locales.

Example: =FINDB("A", A2) returns the byte position of "A".
FIXED:
Rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places and returns it as formatted text, optionally with thousands separators. Use it to embed a formatted number inside a text formula without changing the cell's number format.

Example: =FIXED(1234567.89, 2) returns "1,234,567.89".
LEFT:
Returns the specified number of characters from the start of a text string. Use it to extract a prefix - for instance, the first three characters of a product code.

Example: =LEFT(A2, 3) returns the first 3 characters of A2.
LEFTB:
Like LEFT but counts characters in bytes. Use it in East Asian locales where double-byte characters each take two bytes.

Example: =LEFTB(A2, 4) returns the leftmost 4 bytes.
LEN:
Returns the number of characters in a text string. Use it to check entry lengths - validating that a field isn't too long, detecting trailing spaces, or building dynamic formulas that depend on string length.

Example: =LEN(A2) returns the character count.
LENB:
Returns the number of bytes in a text string, counting double-byte characters as two. Use it in East Asian locales to measure byte length rather than character count.

Example: =LENB(A2) returns the byte length.
LOWER:
Converts all letters in a text string to lowercase. Use it to normalize text before comparing or storing it - for instance, ensuring all email addresses are consistently lowercase.

Example: =LOWER("HELLO WORLD") returns "hello world".
MID:
Returns a specified number of characters from a text string, starting at a given position. Use it to extract a substring from the middle of text - pulling the area code from a phone number or a segment from a fixed-format code.

Example: =MID(A2, 5, 3) returns 3 characters starting at position 5.
MIDB:
Like MID but counts positions and lengths in bytes rather than characters. Used for double-byte character processing in East Asian locales.

Example: =MIDB(A2, 3, 4) returns 4 bytes starting at byte position 3.
NUMBERVALUE:
Converts a number stored as text to an actual number, letting you specify the decimal and thousands separators. Use it when importing data from locales that use commas as decimal separators.

Example: =NUMBERVALUE("1.234,56", ",", ".") returns 1234.56.
PROPER:
Capitalizes the first letter of each word in a text string and lowercases the rest. Use it to standardize names and titles that arrived in inconsistent case - all-caps, all-lowercase, or random mixed case.

Example: =PROPER("john smith") returns "John Smith".
REGEXEXTRACT:
Extracts the first substring that matches a regular expression pattern from a text string. Use it to pull structured data out of free-form text - phone numbers, postal codes, dates - without complex nested formulas.

Example: =REGEXEXTRACT(A2, "\d{5}") returns the first 5-digit sequence in A2.
REGEXREPLACE:
Replaces substrings that match a regular expression pattern with a replacement string. Use it for advanced find-and-replace operations that plain SUBSTITUTE can't handle - removing all punctuation, replacing multiple patterns at once, or manipulating structured text.

Example: =REGEXREPLACE(A2, "[^a-zA-Z0-9]", "") strips all non-alphanumeric characters.
REGEXTEST:
Returns TRUE if a text string contains a match for a regular expression pattern. Use it to validate data formats - checking whether a cell holds a valid email, phone number, or date pattern - without extracting the value.

Example: =REGEXTEST(A2, "^\d{5}(-\d{4})?$") returns TRUE for a valid US ZIP code.
REPLACE:
Replaces a specified number of characters at a given position in a text string with a new string. Use it when you know the exact position of what you want to replace - updating a fixed-format code field or inserting a character at a known offset.

Example: =REPLACE(A2, 4, 2, "XX") replaces characters 4–5 with "XX".
REPLACEB:
Like REPLACE but counts positions and lengths in bytes. Used for double-byte character processing in East Asian locales.

Example: =REPLACEB(A2, 3, 2, "AB") replaces 2 bytes starting at byte 3.
REPT:
Repeats a text string a specified number of times. Use it to build simple in-cell bar charts, pad strings to a fixed width, or create repeated separator patterns inside a formula.

Example: =REPT("|", A2) creates a bar whose length matches the value in A2.
RIGHT:
Returns the specified number of characters from the end of a text string. Use it to extract a suffix - the last four digits of an account number, a file extension, or a trailing code.

Example: =RIGHT(A2, 4) returns the last 4 characters.
RIGHTB:
Like RIGHT but counts characters in bytes. Used in East Asian locale text processing.

Example: =RIGHTB(A2, 4) returns the rightmost 4 bytes.
SEARCH:
Returns the position of one text string within another, case-insensitively, with support for the wildcards ? (any single character) and * (any sequence). Use it for flexible substring searches when the exact capitalization of the target may vary.

Example: =SEARCH("ap*e", A2) finds "apple", "ape", or "Appetite" in A2.
SEARCHB:
Like SEARCH but counts positions in bytes for East Asian locales.

Example: =SEARCHB("A", A2) returns the byte position of the first case-insensitive match of "A".
SUBSTITUTE:
Replaces all occurrences (or a specific occurrence) of a substring within a text string. Use it for formula-driven find-and-replace when you don't know the position - replacing spaces with underscores, removing unwanted characters, or swapping out a code value.

Example: =SUBSTITUTE(A2, " ", "_") replaces all spaces with underscores.
T:
Returns the text value in a cell, or an empty string if the cell doesn't contain text. Use it as a type gate to pass only text values through a formula while silently converting numbers and other types to an empty string.

Example: =T(A2) returns A2's text if it is text, otherwise "".
TEXT:
Formats a number as text using a custom number-format code. Use it to embed a formatted number inside a text string - for example, to display a date or currency amount with the formatting intact when concatenating with other text.

Example: ="Revenue: "&TEXT(A2, "$#,##0.00") produces "Revenue: $1,234.56".
TEXTAFTER:
Returns the text that occurs after a specified delimiter, with options to select which occurrence and how to handle a missing match. Use it to split text without knowing the character position - extracting the domain from an email address, for instance.

Example: =TEXTAFTER(A2, "@") returns everything after the first "@".
TEXTBEFORE:
Returns the text that occurs before a specified delimiter. The counterpart of TEXTAFTER - use it to extract the portion of a string that comes before a known separator.

Example: =TEXTBEFORE(A2, "@") returns the username portion of an email address.
TEXTJOIN:
Joins a range of text values with a specified delimiter, optionally skipping blank cells. Use it to build a comma-separated list, a readable sentence, or any delimited string from a column or row of values.

Example: =TEXTJOIN(", ", TRUE, A2:A10) produces "Alice, Bob, Carol" (skipping any blanks).
TEXTSPLIT:
Splits a text string into a two-dimensional array using row and/or column delimiters. Use it to parse delimited data - splitting a CSV line into separate columns or a multi-line block into rows - in a single formula that spills automatically.

Example: =TEXTSPLIT(A2, ",") splits comma-separated values into individual cells.
TRIM:
Removes leading and trailing spaces from a text string and collapses internal multiple spaces to single spaces. Use it to clean up data entered or imported with extra whitespace, which prevents correct matching, sorting, and lookups.

Example: =TRIM(A2) normalizes spacing in the text.
UNICHAR:
Returns the Unicode character corresponding to a given code point. Use it to insert any Unicode character - symbols, accented letters, emojis - by their code number when you can't type them directly.

Example: =UNICHAR(9829) returns "♥".
UNICODE:
Returns the Unicode code point of the first character in a text string. Use it alongside UNICHAR to inspect or transform individual Unicode characters.

Example: =UNICODE("A") returns 65; =UNICODE("♥") returns 9829.
UPPER:
Converts all letters in a text string to uppercase. Use it to normalize text for display or comparison - ensuring all ticker symbols, country codes, or identifiers are consistently uppercase.

Example: =UPPER("hello world") returns "HELLO WORLD".
VALUE:
Converts a text string that looks like a number into an actual numeric value. Use it when numbers are stored as text - a common problem after imports - causing arithmetic to fail or sort incorrectly.

Example: =VALUE("1,234.56") returns 1234.56.
VALUETOTEXT:
Converts any value - including arrays, errors, and logical values - to its text representation in a concise or strict format. Use it to display or inspect a value as a string, especially for debugging or generating readable output from dynamic arrays.

Example: =VALUETOTEXT(TRUE, 0) returns "TRUE".
